attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine prenatal visit at 28 weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy. She has attended regular antenatal appointments, and all tests performed at her first antenatal visit were unremarkable. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She has no significant medical history and currently takes only multivitamins. Her family history includes a parent with hypertension. Her body mass index is 22 kg/m2. Her blood group is B positive.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate combination of tests to perform at this visit?
options: Full blood count, iron studies and oral glucose test
Full blood count, oral glucose tolerance test, and syphilis serology
Oral glucose tolerance test and Group B streptococcus culture
Syphilis serology, polymerase chain reaction for chlamydia and gonorrhoea and oral glucose test
Full blood count and urine drug screen
attributes.explanation:
The most appropriate combination of tests at 28 weeks gestation includes full blood count, oral glucose tolerance test, and syphilis serology. Current Australian guidelines recommend universal screening for gestational diabetes mellitus between 24 and 28 weeks gestation using an oral glucose tolerance test. Additionally, updated guidelines now recommend universal syphilis screening at both the first antenatal visit and again at 28 weeks gestation for all pregnant individuals, regardless of risk factors. A full blood count is also appropriate to monitor for anaemia, which is common in pregnancy.

Full blood count, iron studies and oral glucose test would be incomplete testing at this visit. While a full blood count is appropriate to monitor for anaemia, iron studies are not routinely performed at 28 weeks unless there is concern for iron deficiency anaemia. More importantly, this option omits the essential syphilis testing that should be performed at this visit. Additionally, the term "oral glucose test" is ambiguous - the proper test for gestational diabetes screening is specifically an oral glucose tolerance test.

Oral glucose tolerance test and Group B streptococcus culture is not the most appropriate combination. While the oral glucose tolerance test is indicated at 28 weeks, Group B streptococcus screening is recommended between 35 and 37 weeks gestation, as colonization status can change over time. Testing at 28 weeks is too early and could lead to false negative results by the time of delivery. This option also omits the full blood count which is appropriate at this visit.

Syphilis serology, polymerase chain reaction for chlamydia and gonorrhoea and oral glucose test is not the most appropriate combination. While syphilis testing is indicated at 28 weeks, repeat testing for chlamydia and gonorrhoea is not routinely recommended unless there are new risk factors or exposures. This option also omits the full blood count which is appropriate at this visit.

Full blood count and urine drug screen is not appropriate. While a full blood count is reasonable, urine drug screening is not routinely performed in pregnancy and is reserved for those with a history or suspicion of substance use. This option also omits the essential gestational diabetes screening and syphilis testing.
learningPoints: Routine screening for gestational diabetes mellitus is recommended for all pregnant individuals at 24–28 weeks gestation.
Current Australian guidelines recommend universal syphilis screening at the first antenatal visit and again at 28 weeks gestation.
Group B streptococcus screening is performed later in pregnancy, typically at 35–37 weeks gestation.
references: Laurie JG, McIntyre HD. A Review of the Current Status of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus in Australia-The Clinical Impact of Changing Population Demographics and Diagnostic Criteria on Prevalence. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2020 Dec 15;17(24):9387. doi: 10.3390/ijerph17249387. PMID: 33333879; PMCID: PMC7765268.
Hyperglycaemia in pregnancy (including gestational diabetes) [published 2019 Jan]. In: eTG complete [digital]. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; 2021 Mar. <https://www.tg.org.au>

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Routine antenatal assessment in the absence of pregnancy complications. https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Routine-antenatal-assessment-in-the-absence-of-pregnancy-complications.pdf

Queensland Clinical Guidelines. Syphilis and pregnancy [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2024 Dec [cited 2025 May 10]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0035/736883/g-sip.pdf
